origin: A biblical genealogy in Ancient Greek, likely the opening sequence of the genealogy in the Gospel of Matthew (lineage from Abraham through Isaac and Jacob to Judah and his brothers).
author: Most plausibly the author/redactor of the Gospel of Matthew (traditionally identified as Matthew the Evangelist), or a later copyist/translator faithfully reproducing that passage in Greek.
thoughts: The author’s perspective is genealogical and providential—presenting ancestral succession as historically grounded and theologically meaningful. The motivation is to establish a legitimate, covenant-based lineage (Abraham → patriarchs → Judah) that supports a larger claim about Jesus’ rightful connection to Israel’s story and promises.
audience: Early Christian readers (and/or Jewish-Christian communities) familiar with Scripture, who would recognize the patriarchal lineage and find comfort and legitimacy in Jesus’ connection to Israel’s covenant history.
written_when: Typically dated to the late 1st century CE (often around ~80–90 CE), though exact dating varies by scholarly view.
